goals: Expose a single `encode_prompt(clip, text)` function that wraps ComfyUI's native CLIP encoder without
Ensure prompt-weighting syntax (`(word:1.3)`) is passed through unmodified and handled by ComfyUI
Keep the module import-safe on CPU-only environments with no model files present.
Return the raw conditioning object produced by ComfyUI (no wrapping), so it is directly passable to
Keep static analysis (ruff, mypy) green with no regressions.

userStories.title: Encode a text prompt to a conditioning tensor
userStories.description: As a library consumer (or an internal pycomfy module), I want to call `encode_prompt(clip, text)` so that I receive a conditioning object ready to pass directly to ComfyUI's sampler.

acceptanceCriteria.text: `from pycomfy.conditioning import encode_prompt` imports without error on CPU with no model files.
`encode_prompt(clip, "a portrait of a woman, studio lighting")` returns a non-`None` value.
The returned object is the raw conditioning output from ComfyUI's CLIP encoder (no additional wrapping).
No ComfyUI loaders or `torch` are imported as a side effect of `import pycomfy.conditioning`.
`ruff check .` and `mypy pycomfy/` pass with no new errors.

userStories.title: Positive and negative prompts share the same function
userStories.description: As a library consumer, I want `encode_prompt` to work identically for both positive and negative prompts so that I am responsible for the distinction and the API stays minimal.

acceptanceCriteria.text: Calling `encode_prompt(clip, "ugly, blurry")` (negative) returns a non-`None` value using the same
No separate `encode_negative_prompt` function or `is_negative` parameter is added to the public API.

userStories.title: Prompt weighting syntax passes through unchanged
userStories.description: As a library consumer, I want weighted prompts like `"a portrait of a woman, (studio lighting:1.3)"` to be accepted by `encode_prompt` without modification so that ComfyUI's native weighting logic applies.

acceptanceCriteria.text: `encode_prompt(clip, "a portrait of a woman, (studio lighting:1.3)")` returns a non-`None` value.
pycomfy does not parse, strip, or transform the prompt string before passing it to the ComfyUI encoder.
No exception is raised for valid ComfyUI weighting syntax.

userStories.title: Empty string encodes without crashing
userStories.description: As a library consumer, I want `encode_prompt(clip, "")` to succeed so that the common unconditional / empty negative prompt case works without special-casing.

acceptanceCriteria.text: No exception is raised for an empty string input.

userStories.title: CPU-only import never crashes
userStories.description: As a developer running CI (CPU-only, no model files), I want `from pycomfy.conditioning import encode_prompt` to succeed so that the module can be tested and linted in CI without GPU or model files.

acceptanceCriteria.text: Running `uv run python -c "from pycomfy.conditioning import encode_prompt; print('ok')"` exits with
No `torch`, `comfy.sd`, or any heavy loader is imported at module import time.

functionalRequirements.description: `pycomfy/conditioning.py` exposes a public function `encode_prompt(clip: Any, text: str) -> Any`.
`encode_prompt` calls ComfyUI's CLIP encoder using the two-call pattern from `CLIPTextEncode`
The prompt string is passed to the ComfyUI encoder unchanged — no pre-processing, stripping, or
`encode_prompt` accepts an empty string (`""`) and returns a valid conditioning object.
`pycomfy/conditioning.py` must not import `torch`, `comfy.sd`, or any model loader at module level.
`encode_prompt` is exported from `pycomfy/conditioning.py` and optionally re-exported from
Type hints use `Any` for `clip` and the return type, consistent with the pattern established in
